preamble.summary: Elihu addresses Job directly with his first sustained argument: God speaks to human beings, but they do not perceive it. God speaks through dreams and night visions, terrifying people to turn them from destructive paths. God also speaks through suffering — through pain on the sickbed, when a person wastes away and draws near the pit. But then, if there is a mediating angel, one among a thousand, to declare what is right for that person, God is gracious and says, 'Deliver him from going down to the pit — I have found a ransom.' The person's flesh is restored, he returns to the days of his youth, he prays and God accepts him, and he comes before others declaring, 'I sinned and twisted what was right, but it was not repaid to me.' God redeems his soul from the pit, and his life sees light. Elihu concludes by inviting Job to respond or, if he has nothing to say, to listen further.
preamble.remarkable: Job 33 contains one of the most theologically advanced passages in the Hebrew Bible. In verses 23-28, Elihu describes a process of suffering, intercession, ransom, and restoration that anticipates major themes in later biblical theology. The malakh melits ('mediating angel' or 'interpreting messenger') who declares what is right for the sufferer, the kofer ('ransom') that God finds to deliver from the pit, and the padah ('redemption') of the soul — these concepts form a constellation that later tradition will connect to atonement theology. What makes Elihu's contribution distinctive is that he reframes suffering not as punishment (the friends' view) or as inexplicable injustice (Job's view) but as communication. God uses suffering to speak — to open ears, to redirect lives, to create the conditions in which a person can hear what comfort and prosperity would have drowned out. This is not a complete theodicy, but it is the first voice in the dialogue that offers suffering a purpose beyond retribution.
preamble.friction: Elihu begins by quoting Job's own words back to him (vv. 8-11) and then says, 'In this you are not right' (v. 12). This is more honest and more respectful than anything the three friends did — Elihu engages Job's actual arguments rather than attacking a straw man. However, the quotation is selective: Elihu focuses on Job's claim of total innocence and God's hostility but ignores Job's more nuanced protests about legal process and the absence of a mediator. Ironically, Elihu then offers exactly the mediator figure Job longed for in 9:33 (a mokiach, 'arbiter') and 16:19 (a witness in heaven) — but Elihu does not seem to realize he is answering Job's earlier prayers. The mediating angel of verse 23 is the theological answer to Job's courtroom metaphor, but Elihu presents it as his own insight rather than as a response to Job's request.
preamble.connections: The malakh melits ('mediating angel') of verse 23 connects to Job's yearning for an arbiter (mokiach) in 9:33 and a witness/advocate (ed/melits) in 16:19-21. The kofer ('ransom') of verse 24 uses the same root as the kippurim ('atonements') of Leviticus 16 and anticipates the ransom (lutron) language of Mark 10:45. The restoration sequence — flesh renewed, youth restored, prayer accepted, sin confessed, soul redeemed — parallels the jubilee theology of Leviticus 25 and the restoration promises of the prophets. The phrase 'his life shall see light' (v. 28) connects to the Servant Song of Isaiah 53:11 ('he shall see light and be satisfied'). Elihu's theology of suffering as divine communication anticipates Hebrews 12:5-11, which interprets suffering as the discipline of a loving father.

verses.text_hebrew: וְאוּלָ֗ם שְׁמַע־נָ֣א אִיּ֣וֹב מִלָּ֑י וְכׇל־דְּ֝בָרַ֗י הַאֲזִֽינָה׃
verses.text_kjv: Wherefore, Job, I pray thee, hear my speeches, and hearken to all my words.
verses.rendering:
But now, Job — hear my words.
Listen to everything I say.
translator_notes: Elihu addresses Job by name — the first time any speaker in the dialogue has done so. The friends spoke about Job in the third person or addressed him with 'you.' Elihu's direct address signals a different kind of engagement: personal, face-to-face, naming the man he is about to challenge.

verses.text_hebrew: הִנֵּה־נָ֭א פָּתַ֣חְתִּי פִ֑י דִּבְּרָ֖ה לְשׁוֹנִ֣י בְחִכִּֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Behold, now I have opened my mouth, my tongue hath spoken in my mouth.
verses.rendering:
Look — I have opened my mouth;
my tongue speaks against my palate.
translator_notes: The verb patachti ('I have opened') echoes Job's patach in 3:1 and Elihu's promised opening in 32:20. The phrase dibbrah leshoni bechikki ('my tongue has spoken in my palate') gives a physical description of speech: the tongue pressing against the roof of the mouth to form words. Elihu is hyper-aware of the act of speaking — every verse of his introduction is about the mechanics and morality of utterance.

verses.text_hebrew: יֹֽשֶׁר־לִבִּ֥י אֲמָרָ֑י וְדַ֥עַת שְׂ֝פָתַ֗י בָּר֥וּר מִלֵּֽלוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: My words shall be of the uprightness of my heart: and my lips shall utter knowledge clearly.
verses.rendering:
My words come from an upright heart,
and my lips speak knowledge sincerely.
translator_notes: The yosher libbi ('the uprightness of my heart') amarai ('my words') — Elihu claims his speech originates in moral integrity, not intellectual posturing. The parallel ve-da'at sefatai barur millelu ('and knowledge my lips clearly speak') emphasizes clarity: barur ('clear, pure, refined') suggests words that have been purified of deception. Elihu stakes his credibility on sincerity and transparency.

verses.text_hebrew: רֽוּחַ־אֵ֥ל עָשָׂ֑תְנִי וְנִשְׁמַ֖ת שַׁדַּ֣י תְּחַיֵּֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: The Spirit of God hath made me, and the breath of the Almighty hath given me life.
verses.rendering:
The spirit of God made me,
and the breath of the Almighty gives me life.
verses.expanded_rendering: Elihu restates his theological foundation from 32:8: ruach El asatni ('the spirit of God made me'). He is a creature of God's breath, animated by the same force that hovered over the waters in Genesis 1:2. The parallel venishmat Shaddai techayeni ('and the breath of the Almighty gives me life') uses the verb chayah ('to live, to give life') — Elihu is alive because God breathes. This is not a claim to prophetic authority but a claim to shared creaturely status with Job. The implicit argument: I am made of the same stuff you are, by the same God. We stand on equal ground.
translator_notes: This verse echoes Genesis 2:7 almost directly: God formed man and breathed into his nostrils the neshamah of life. Elihu uses this shared origin to establish credibility and equality — he is not claiming to be above Job but alongside him, fellow creature before the same Creator.

verses.text_hebrew: אִם־תּוּכַ֥ל הֲשִׁיבֵ֑נִי עֶרְכָ֥ה לְ֝פָנַ֗י הִתְיַצָּֽבָה׃
verses.text_kjv: If thou canst answer me, set thy words in order before me, stand up.
verses.rendering:
If you can, answer me.
Set your case in order before me; take your stand.
translator_notes: Elihu invites rebuttal: im tukal hashiveni ('if you are able, answer me'). The verb arkhah ('set in order, arrange') is a legal term for marshaling arguments in a court case — the same courtroom language Job himself has used. The verb hityatstsavah ('take your stand, present yourself') suggests a formal legal posture. Elihu frames the exchange as a fair hearing, not a lecture.

verses.text_hebrew: הֶן־אֲנִ֣י כְפִ֣יךָ לָאֵ֑ל מֵ֝חֹ֗מֶר קֹרַ֥צְתִּי גַם־אָֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: Behold, I am according to thy wish in God's stead: I also am formed out of the clay.
verses.rendering:
I stand before God just as you do.
I too was pinched from clay.
verses.expanded_rendering: Elihu establishes radical equality: hen ani khefikha la-El ('I am like your mouth toward God' or 'I am according to your wish before God'). The interpretation is debated, but the thrust is clear — Elihu occupies the same position before God that Job does. The second line removes any doubt: me-chomer qoratstti gam ani ('from clay I too was pinched/nipped off'). The verb qarats ('to pinch, to nip off') is a potter's term — God pinched off a piece of clay and formed Elihu, just as God formed Adam from the earth. Elihu is not divine, not angelic, not superior. He is mud, shaped by the same hands that shaped Job.
translator_notes: The verb qarats ('to pinch off') is rare and evocative — it suggests the specific action of a potter tearing a piece of clay from a larger mass to begin forming a vessel. This is more intimate than the general 'formed' (yatsar) of Genesis 2:7. Elihu's self-description is humble: he is a torn-off piece of earth.

verses.text_hebrew: הִנֵּ֣ה אֵ֭מָתִי לֹ֣א תְבַעֲתֶ֑ךָּ וְ֝אַכְפִּ֗י עָלֶ֥יךָ לֹא־יִכְבָּֽד׃
verses.text_kjv: Behold, my terror shall not make thee afraid, neither shall my hand be heavy upon thee.
verses.rendering:
My presence will not terrify you,
and my pressure on you will not be heavy.
verses.expanded_rendering: This verse directly answers Job's complaint in 9:34 and 13:21 — Job said he could not argue with God because God's dread (eimah) crushed him and God's hand (kaf) was heavy upon him. Elihu says: eimati lo tevaathekkha ('my dread will not terrify you') — I am not God; you need not fear me. And ve-akhpi aleikha lo yikhbad ('and my pressure upon you will not be heavy') — I will not crush you with overwhelming power. Elihu is offering what Job asked for: a fair hearing with an opponent who does not terrify the defendant into silence. He is the human-scale interlocutor Job needed.
translator_notes: The word eimah ('terror, dread') and the verb ba'at ('to terrify') are the same terms Job used when describing his inability to face God in court (9:34, 13:21). Elihu's awareness of Job's specific vocabulary suggests he has been listening carefully to the entire dialogue.

verses.text_hebrew: אַ֭ךְ אָמַ֣רְתָּ בְאׇזְנָ֑י וְק֖וֹל מִלִּ֣ין אֶשְׁמָֽע׃
verses.text_kjv: Surely thou hast spoken in mine hearing, and I have heard the voice of thy words, saying,
verses.rendering:
But you have said in my hearing —
yes, I heard the very sound of your words:
translator_notes: Elihu now turns from introduction to argument. The phrase amarta be-oznai ('you said in my ears') emphasizes that Elihu is not relying on hearsay — he heard Job with his own ears. The qol millin eshma ('the sound of words I heard') doubles down: this is direct testimony. Elihu will now quote Job, and the quotation, while selective, is not fabricated.

verses.text_hebrew: זַ֥ךְ אֲנִ֗י בְּלִ֫י פָ֥שַׁע חַ֥ף אָנֹכִ֑י וְלֹ֖א עָוֺ֣ן לִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: I am clean without transgression, I am innocent; neither is there iniquity in me.
verses.rendering:
'I am pure, without transgression.
I am clean; there is no guilt in me.'
verses.expanded_rendering: Elihu quotes Job's claim of innocence using three near-synonyms: zakh ('pure, clean, clear') — the purity of refined metal or clarified oil; beli fasha ('without transgression') — free of rebellion against God; chaf ('innocent, clean') — washed of all wrongdoing. The triple assertion covers every possible category of sin: ritual impurity, willful rebellion, and moral guilt. Elihu is summarizing, not quoting verbatim — Job never made this exact statement in these exact words, but he did claim innocence repeatedly (9:21, 10:7, 16:17, 23:10-12, 27:5-6). Elihu's summary is fair in substance if not in precise wording.
translator_notes: The word zakh ('pure') is from the language of metallurgy and oil-pressing — it means refined, free of impurities. The word chaf ('innocent') means 'washed clean.' Together with beli fasha ('without rebellion'), these cover the full spectrum of Job's innocence claims. Elihu does not dispute Job's moral character — he disputes the theological conclusion Job draws from it.

verses.text_hebrew: הֵ֣ן תְּ֭נוּאוֹת עָלַ֣י יִמְצָ֑א יַחְשְׁבֵ֖נִי לְאוֹיֵ֣ב לֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: Behold, he findeth occasions against me, he counteth me for his enemy;
verses.rendering:
'But God finds pretexts against me;
he counts me as his enemy.'
translator_notes: Elihu continues quoting Job. The word tenu'ot ('pretexts, occasions for hostility, charges') suggests trumped-up accusations — God is looking for excuses to punish Job. The verb yachsheveni ('he counts me, he reckons me') le-oyev lo ('as an enemy to him') echoes Job 13:24 and 19:11 where Job accused God of treating him as a foe. Elihu has identified the core of Job's complaint: not that God punishes sin, but that God persecutes innocence.

verses.text_hebrew: יָשֵׂ֣ם בַּ֭סַּד רַגְלַ֑י יִ֝שְׁמֹ֗ר כׇּל־אׇרְחֹתָֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: He putteth my feet in the stocks, he marketh all my paths.
verses.rendering:
'He puts my feet in the stocks;
he watches all my paths.'
translator_notes: The third element of Elihu's quotation: yasem bassad raglai ('he places my feet in the stocks') echoes Job 13:27 almost verbatim. The sad ('stocks, wooden block') is a restraining device for prisoners. The yishmor kol orchotai ('he watches all my paths') echoes the same verse — God as warden, tracking every movement. Elihu has accurately captured Job's image of God as jailer.

verses.text_hebrew: הֶן־זֹ֣את לֹא־צָדַ֣קְתָּ אֶעֱנֶ֑ךָּ כִּֽי־יִרְבֶּ֥ה אֱ֝ל֗וֹהַ מֵאֱנֽוֹשׁ׃
verses.text_kjv: Behold, in this thou art not just: I will answer thee, that God is greater than man.
verses.rendering:
In this you are not right — let me answer you:
God is greater than any mortal.
verses.expanded_rendering: Elihu delivers his verdict on Job's complaint: hen zot lo tsadaqta ('in this you are not justified'). The word tsadaqta from tsadaq is the same root Job has been using to claim righteousness — Elihu turns it back on him. Job may be morally innocent, but his conclusion about God is wrong. The reason: ki yirbeh Eloah me-enosh ('for God is greater than a human being'). The verb yirbeh ('is greater, is more, exceeds') establishes an asymmetry: God and humans do not operate on the same plane. Job's error, in Elihu's analysis, is not moral guilt but theological overreach — he has tried to hold God to human standards of accountability.
translator_notes: This is Elihu's thesis statement. He does not say Job is wicked (as the friends did) or that Job's suffering is deserved. He says Job is wrong about God — specifically, wrong to demand that God explain Himself on human terms. The phrase me-enosh ('than a mortal') uses enosh, the term for humanity in its weakness and mortality, emphasizing the gap between Creator and creature.

verses.text_hebrew: מַ֭דּוּעַ אֵלָ֣יו רִיב֑וֹתָ כִּ֥י כׇל־דְּ֝בָרָ֗יו לֹ֣א יַעֲנֶֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Why dost thou strive against him? for he giveth not account of any of his matters.
verses.rendering:
Why do you contend against him?
He does not answer for any of his actions.
translator_notes: The verb rivota ('you contend, you bring a lawsuit') is legal language — Job has been suing God, and Elihu asks why. The reason God does not respond to Job's lawsuit is not indifference but sovereignty: kol devarav lo ya'aneh ('all his words/matters he does not answer'). God is not accountable to human courts. This sounds like it could slide into the friends' authoritarianism, but Elihu's next move is different: he will argue that God does speak — just not in the courtroom Job has constructed.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּי־בְ֭אַחַת יְדַבֶּר־אֵ֑ל וּ֝בִשְׁתַּ֗יִם לֹ֣א יְשׁוּרֶֽנָּה׃
verses.text_kjv: For God speaketh once, yea twice, yet man perceiveth it not.
verses.rendering:
For God speaks in one way,
and in two — though no one perceives it.
verses.expanded_rendering: The pivot of the chapter. Elihu's argument shifts from 'God does not answer' to 'God does speak, but you are not listening.' The formula be-achat ... u-vishtayim ('in one way ... and in two') is a Hebrew numerical ladder (X, X+1) meaning 'in multiple ways, again and again.' God speaks repeatedly through various channels. The devastating conclusion: lo yeshurenah ('no one perceives it, no one notices'). The verb shur ('to see, to observe, to perceive') implies that God's speech requires eyes, not ears — it must be discerned, not merely heard. Job has been demanding that God speak in a courtroom; Elihu will argue that God has been speaking all along through dreams and through suffering.
translator_notes: The numerical ladder pattern (one ... two) is common in Hebrew poetry (Proverbs 6:16, 30:15, 30:18, Amos 1:3). It does not mean exactly two; it means 'multiple, more than you expect.' Elihu will describe two specific channels: dreams (vv. 15-18) and pain (vv. 19-22).

verses.text_hebrew: בַּחֲל֥וֹם חֶזְי֗וֹן לַ֫יְלָ֥ה בִּנְפֹ֣ל תַּ֭רְדֵּמָה עַל־אֲנָשִׁ֑ים בִּ֝תְנוּמ֗וֹת עֲלֵ֣י מִשְׁכָּֽב׃
verses.text_kjv: In a dream, in a vision of the night, when deep sleep falleth upon men, in slumberings upon the bed;
verses.rendering:
In a dream, in a vision of the night,
when deep sleep falls upon people,
in slumber on the bed —
verses.expanded_rendering: The first channel of divine speech: the dream. The word chalom ('dream') paired with chezyon lailah ('vision of the night') covers both ordinary dreams and prophetic visions. The tardemah ('deep sleep') is the same word used for the supernatural sleep God cast on Adam (Genesis 2:21) and on Abraham (Genesis 15:12) — this is not ordinary sleep but God-induced unconsciousness, the state in which humans are most receptive because their defenses are down. The tenu'mot ('slumberings') alei mishkav ('upon the bed') locates the encounter: in the most vulnerable place, at the most vulnerable hour, when the conscious mind has released its grip.
translator_notes: The tardemah ('deep sleep') is consistently associated with divine action in the Hebrew Bible: God puts Adam into tardemah to create Eve, puts Abraham into tardemah to make the covenant, causes tardemah to fall on Saul's camp so David can escape. It is not natural sleep but supernaturally deepened unconsciousness — the state in which God can work without human interference.

verses.text_hebrew: אָ֣ז יִ֭גְלֶה אֹ֣זֶן אֲנָשִׁ֑ים וּבְמֹסָרָ֥ם יַחְתֹּֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: Then he openeth the ears of men, and sealeth their instruction,
verses.rendering:
then he opens people's ears
and seals his instruction upon them,
verses.expanded_rendering: What God does in the dream: yigleh ozen anashim ('he uncovers the ear of people'). The idiom galah ozen ('to uncover the ear') means to reveal, to disclose, to whisper something confidential. It implies intimacy — God bends close to the sleeper's ear and removes whatever was blocking it. The parallel u-vemosaram yachtom ('and in their instruction he seals') uses chatam ('to seal') as in sealing a document — God stamps the instruction permanently, making it official and irrevocable. The dream is not a passing fancy but a sealed decree, a divine communique with the authority of a royal seal.
translator_notes: The phrase galah ozen ('uncover the ear') appears also in 1 Samuel 9:15 ('the LORD had uncovered Samuel's ear') and 2 Samuel 7:27 ('you, LORD, have uncovered the ear of your servant'). It is the language of prophetic revelation. Elihu claims that ordinary people receive prophetic-quality communication from God in dreams.

verses.text_hebrew: לְ֭הָסִיר אָדָ֣ם מַעֲשֶׂ֑ה וְ֝גֵוָ֗ה מִגֶּ֥בֶר יְכַסֶּֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: That he may withdraw man from his purpose, and hide pride from man.
verses.rendering:
to turn a person from a deed,
to cut away pride from a man.
translator_notes: The purpose of the dream: lehasir adam ma'aseh ('to turn a person away from a deed') — to redirect someone from a destructive course of action. The parallel ve-gevah mi-gever yekhsasseh ('and pride from a man he covers/hides') uses the verb kasah ('to cover, to hide, to remove') — God covers pride, buries it, removes it from the person's operating system. Dreams are preventive medicine: they stop bad actions before they happen and strip away the arrogance that drives them.

verses.text_hebrew: יַחְשֹׂ֣ךְ נַ֭פְשׁוֹ מִנִּי־שָׁ֑חַת וְ֝חַיָּת֗וֹ מֵעֲבֹ֥ר בַּשָּֽׁלַח׃
verses.text_kjv: He keepeth back his soul from the pit, and his life from perishing by the sword.
verses.rendering:
He holds his soul back from the pit
and his life from crossing over into death.
verses.expanded_rendering: The ultimate purpose: yachsokh nafsho minni shachat ('he withholds his soul from the pit'). The shachat ('pit') is both a literal grave and a metaphor for Sheol, the realm of the dead. The parallel ve-chayyato me'avor ba-shelach ('and his life from passing through the channel/weapon') is textually difficult — shelach may mean 'weapon' (a thrown javelin), 'channel' (a waterway), or a euphemism for death's passage. Either way, the meaning is clear: the dream saves a life. God intervenes in sleep to prevent a waking catastrophe. This is the first channel of divine speech: prevention through revelation.
translator_notes: The word shachat ('pit') appears throughout Job as a synonym for death and Sheol (9:31, 17:14). It will appear again in verses 22, 24, 28, and 30 of this chapter, forming the structural keyword of Elihu's argument: the pit is the destination God is trying to prevent.

verses.text_hebrew: וְהוּכַ֣ח בְּ֭מַכְאוֹב עַל־מִשְׁכָּב֑וֹ וְרִ֖יב עֲצָמָ֣יו אֵתָֽן׃
verses.text_kjv: He is chastened also with pain upon his bed, and the multitude of his bones with strong pain:
verses.rendering:
Or a person is rebuked with pain on the sickbed,
with unceasing conflict in the bones —
verses.expanded_rendering: The second channel of divine speech: suffering. The verb hukach ('he is reproved, he is corrected') from yakach — the same verb used for legal argument throughout Job — reframes pain as communication. The makh'ov ('pain, suffering') al mishkavo ('upon his bed') is the same bed where dreams come (v. 15), but now the message arrives through the body instead of through visions. The parallel ve-riv atsamav etan ('and the strife of his bones is constant/mighty') uses riv ('strife, contention, lawsuit') — the bones themselves are in dispute, fighting against the person. The word etan ('mighty, constant, perennial') suggests pain that does not let up. This is God's second language: when dreams do not get through, pain speaks.
translator_notes: Elihu's move here is theologically daring. He does not say suffering is punishment for specific sins (the friends' position) but that suffering is a form of divine address — God speaking through the body when the mind would not listen. This reframes the entire debate: Job's suffering may not be retribution but communication.

verses.text_hebrew: וְזִֽהֲמַ֣תּוּ חַיָּת֣וֹ לָ֑חֶם וְ֝נַפְשׁ֗וֹ מַאֲכַ֥ל תַּאֲוָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: So that his life abhorreth bread, and his soul dainty meat.
verses.rendering:
so that his life loathes bread
and his appetite recoils from choice food.
translator_notes: The verb zihammattu ('it makes loathsome, it causes revulsion') from zaham describes visceral disgust — the sick person's body rejects food. The chayyato lachem ('his life bread') and nafsho ma'akhal ta'avah ('his appetite choice food') describe the total collapse of appetite. Even the most desirable food (ma'akhal ta'avah, 'food of desire') becomes nauseating. The body, which should sustain life through eating, now refuses nourishment.

verses.text_hebrew: יִ֣כֶל בְּשָׂר֣וֹ מֵרֹ֑אִי וְשֻׁפּ֖וּ עַצְמוֹתָ֣יו לֹ֣א רֻאּֽוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: His flesh is consumed away, that it cannot be seen; and his bones that were not seen stick out.
verses.rendering:
His flesh wastes away until it cannot be seen,
and his bones, once hidden, protrude.
translator_notes: The verb yikhel ('it is consumed, it wastes away') from kalah describes total depletion. The besaro me-ro'i ('his flesh from sight') — the flesh disappears, consumed by disease. The reversal follows: ve-shuppu atsmotav lo ru'u ('and his bones that were not seen are laid bare'). The bones that flesh once covered now jut out visibly. The body becomes a skeleton while still alive — a walking death. This is a clinical description of severe wasting illness.

verses.text_hebrew: וַתִּקְרַ֣ב לַשַּׁ֣חַת נַפְשׁ֑וֹ וְ֝חַיָּת֗וֹ לַמְמִתִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Yea, his soul draweth near unto the grave, and his life to the destroyers.
verses.rendering:
His soul draws near the pit,
his life approaches the realm of death.
translator_notes: The progression reaches its terminus: vatiqrav la-shachat nafsho ('his soul draws near to the pit'). The shachat ('pit') returns from verse 18 — the very destination God's dream was trying to prevent. The parallel ve-chayyato la-memitim ('and his life to the death-dealers') introduces the memitim ('those who kill, the destroyers') — possibly angels of death, possibly the forces of mortality themselves. The sufferer stands at the edge of Sheol. This is the maximum point of crisis — and it is here that Elihu introduces the mediating angel.

verses.text_hebrew: אִם־יֵ֤שׁ עָלָ֨יו ׀ מַלְאָ֗ךְ מֵלִ֗יץ אֶחָ֥ד מִנִּי־אָ֑לֶף לְהַגִּ֖יד לְאָדָ֣ם יׇשְׁרֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: If there be a messenger with him, an interpreter, one among a thousand, to shew unto man his uprightness;
verses.rendering:
If there is an angel beside him,
a mediator, one among a thousand,
to declare to a person what is right for him —
verses.expanded_rendering: This is the theological climax of the chapter. At the moment of maximum crisis — soul at the edge of the pit — Elihu introduces the malakh melits ('mediating angel, interpreting messenger'). The word malakh means 'messenger' or 'angel,' and melits means 'interpreter, advocate, mediator' — one who stands between two parties and translates, intercedes, explains. This figure is echad minni alef ('one among a thousand'), meaning extraordinarily rare, almost impossible to find. The mediator's task: lehagid le-adam yoshro ('to declare to a person his uprightness' or 'to declare to a person what is right'). The yoshro could mean 'his [the person's] uprightness' — the angel confirms the sufferer's integrity. Or it could mean 'what is upright/right for him' — the angel reveals the correct path. Either way, the angel breaks the silence between God and the sufferer, providing the mediation that Job longed for in 9:33 and 16:19-21.
translator_notes: The malakh melits is one of the most debated figures in Job. Job asked for a mokiach ('arbiter') in 9:33, an ed ('witness') in 16:19, and a go'el ('redeemer') in 19:25. Elihu's mediating angel may be the answer to all three requests — but Elihu presents it as his own theological insight, not as a response to Job's prayers. The phrase 'one among a thousand' emphasizes rarity: such intercession is possible but not guaranteed.

key_terms.semantic_range: malakh = messenger, angel, envoy; melits = interpreter, advocate, mediator, intercessor
key_terms.note: The malakh melits ('interpreting messenger' or 'mediating angel') stands between the dying person and the pit. This figure combines the roles of messenger (one who carries communication between God and humans), interpreter (one who translates divine purpose into human understanding), and advocate (one who argues on behalf of the sufferer). The combination is unique in the Hebrew Bible and represents Elihu's most original theological contribution. Later tradition will see in this figure a prototype of angelic intercession and ultimately of messianic mediation.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיְחֻנֶּ֗נּוּ וַ֭יֹּאמֶר פְּדָעֵ֥הוּ מֵרֶ֥דֶת שָׁ֑חַת מָ֝צָ֗אתִי כֹּֽפֶר׃
verses.text_kjv: Then he is gracious unto him, and saith, Deliver him from going down to the pit: I have found a ransom.
verses.rendering:
then God is gracious to him and says,
'Deliver him from going down to the pit —
I have found a ransom.'
verses.expanded_rendering: The response to the mediator's intercession: vaychunnennu ('and he shows him grace'). The verb channan ('to be gracious, to show favor') is the language of unmerited mercy — God's response to the angel's mediation is not justice but grace. Then God speaks: peda'ehu me-redet shachat ('deliver him from going down to the pit'). The verb pada ('to redeem, to ransom, to deliver') is the language of liberation — setting a prisoner free, buying back a slave, releasing a debtor. And the grounds for this redemption: matsati kofer ('I have found a ransom'). The kofer is the price paid to secure release — a ransom, a covering, a substitute. God himself finds the ransom. The sufferer does not provide it; the mediating angel does not provide it; God provides it. This is grace operating through mediation: the angel intercedes, God responds with mercy, and the ransom is God's own provision.
translator_notes: The kofer ('ransom') is from the same root as kippur ('atonement, covering'). In Exodus 21:30, kofer is the ransom price paid to save a life. In Exodus 30:12, it is the atonement money each Israelite pays. Here, God finds the kofer — the ransom is not a human payment but a divine discovery. This reverses the normal transaction: instead of the guilty party paying to escape punishment, the judge provides the payment to rescue the accused.

key_terms.semantic_range: ransom, ransom price, atonement payment, covering, the price paid to redeem a life
key_terms.note: kofer ('ransom') is from the root k-p-r, the same root that produces kippur ('atonement') and kapporet ('mercy seat'). The fundamental meaning is a payment or covering that averts destruction. In this verse, God declares matsati kofer ('I have found a ransom') — the initiative is divine. God does not wait for the sufferer to produce payment; God finds it. The identity of this ransom is left unstated, which has generated centuries of theological reflection. What does God find that satisfies the demands of justice while extending mercy to the dying? Elihu does not say. The question hangs in the air, awaiting later revelation.

verses.text_hebrew: רֻטֲפַ֣שׁ בְּשָׂר֣וֹ מִנֹּ֑עַר יָ֝שׁ֗וּב לִימֵ֥י עֲלוּמָֽיו׃
verses.text_kjv: His flesh shall be fresher than a child's: he shall return to the days of his youth.
verses.rendering:
His flesh becomes fresher than a child's;
he returns to the days of his youth.
verses.expanded_rendering: The restoration begins immediately. The word rutafash ('is made fresh, is renewed, becomes plump') is a rare verb suggesting the restoration of healthy, youthful flesh — the wasting of verse 21 is reversed. The besaro minno'ar ('his flesh more than a youth's') means the renewed flesh surpasses even a young person's in vitality. The parallel yashuv limei alumav ('he returns to the days of his youth') uses shuv ('to return, to turn back') — the person does not merely recover but regresses to the prime of life. The ransomed body is not merely healed but renewed beyond its original condition.
translator_notes: The verb rutafash occurs only here in the Hebrew Bible, making its exact meaning uncertain. The context demands something like 'made plump, made fresh, restored to healthy fullness.' The return to youth is a reversal of the wasting described in verses 19-22 — what disease consumed, God's ransom restores and then exceeds.

verses.text_hebrew: יֶעְתַּ֣ר אֶל־אֱ֭לוֹהַ וַיִּרְצֵ֑הוּ וַיַּ֥רְא פָ֝נָ֗יו בִּתְרוּעָ֥ה וַיָּ֤שֶׁב ׀ לֶאֱנ֬וֹשׁ צִדְקָתֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: He shall pray unto God, and he will be favourable unto him: and he shall see his face with joy; for he will render unto man his righteousness.
verses.rendering:
He prays to God, and God accepts him.
He sees God's face with a shout of joy,
and God restores his righteousness to him.
verses.expanded_rendering: The restoration now extends from the body to the relationship with God. The ye'tar el Eloah ('he prays/entreats God') uses atar ('to pray, to entreat, to supplicate') — the restored person can approach God again. And vayyirtsehu ('and He accepts him') — the verb ratsah ('to accept, to be pleased with, to take delight in') means God receives the prayer with favor. The climax: vayyar panav bitru'ah ('and he sees His face with a shout of joy'). The sufferer, who was at the edge of the pit, now sees the face of God — the ultimate blessing in Hebrew theology (Numbers 6:25-26). The teru'ah ('shout, trumpet blast, acclamation') is the sound of jubilee, of liberation, of worship. Finally: vayyashev le-enosh tsidqato ('and He restores to the person his righteousness'). The righteousness that seemed lost — that the sufferer doubted, that the friends denied — is returned by God's own declaration.
translator_notes: The sequence — prayer, acceptance, face-to-face encounter, restored righteousness — is a complete salvation narrative compressed into a single verse. The 'seeing of God's face' is the highest possible restoration, since Exodus 33:20 declares that no one can see God's face and live. Elihu describes a person brought back from the edge of death to the fullest possible intimacy with God.

verses.text_hebrew: יָשֹׁ֤ר ׀ עַל־אֲנָשִׁ֗ים וַ֭יֹּאמֶר חָטָ֣אתִי וְיָשָׁ֣ר הֶעֱוֵ֑יתִי וְלֹא־שָׁ֥וָה לִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: He looketh upon men, and if any say, I have sinned, and perverted that which was right, and it profited me not;
verses.rendering:
He sings before others and says,
'I sinned and twisted what was right,
but it was not repaid to me.'
verses.expanded_rendering: The restored person now testifies publicly. The verb yashor ('he sings, he looks upon') is debated — it may mean 'he looks upon' (from shur) or 'he sings' (from shir). The context of public testimony favors singing: the restored person stands before others (al anashim) and confesses. The confession is threefold: chatati ('I sinned') — a straightforward admission of wrongdoing; ve-yashar he'eveti ('and what was straight I twisted') — he perverted the right path; ve-lo shavah li ('and it was not repaid to me, it was not equivalent for me') — the punishment did not match the crime, or rather, the crime did not receive the punishment it deserved. This last phrase is the key: the restored person acknowledges sin but also acknowledges that God's mercy exceeded justice. He got grace, not what he deserved.
translator_notes: The phrase ve-lo shavah li ('it was not repaid to me') is theologically crucial. It means that God did not give the person the punishment their sin warranted. Grace exceeded strict justice. This is the testimony of someone who has experienced kofer (ransom): I was guilty, but God found a covering, and I was restored beyond what I deserved.

verses.text_hebrew: פָּדָ֣ה נַ֭פְשׁוֹ מֵעֲבֹ֣ר בַּשָּׁ֑חַת וְ֝חַיָּתִ֗י בָּא֥וֹר תִּרְאֶֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: He will deliver his soul from going into the pit, and his life shall see the light.
verses.rendering:
He redeemed my soul from crossing into the pit,
and my life will see the light.
verses.expanded_rendering: The testimony concludes with the language of redemption. The verb padah ('he redeemed, he ransomed, he delivered') is the verb of liberation — the same verb used for God's redemption of Israel from Egypt (Deuteronomy 7:8, 13:5). The nafsho me'avor ba-shachat ('his soul from crossing into the pit') echoes verse 18, completing the circle: the pit that threatened to swallow him has been denied its prey. The final declaration shifts to first person: ve-chayyati ba-or tireh ('and my life in the light will see'). The or ('light') that Job cursed in chapter 3 — the light he wanted extinguished — is here the destination of the redeemed life. To see light is to live, to be restored, to stand again in the illuminated world of God's creation. The anti-Genesis of Job 3 is answered by this pro-Genesis restoration.
translator_notes: The shift from third person ('he redeemed his soul') to first person ('my life will see') in many Hebrew manuscripts has caused textual debate. Some read both as third person. The first-person reading is more dramatic — the testimony becomes personal mid-sentence, as if the speaker is overwhelmed by gratitude and drops the formal narration.

key_terms.semantic_range: to ransom, to redeem, to deliver, to buy back, to liberate from bondage or death
key_terms.note: padah ('to redeem') is the great exodus verb — God padah Israel from Egypt (Deuteronomy 7:8). It implies liberation through payment or powerful intervention. In this verse, it summarizes the entire salvific sequence of verses 23-27: the mediating angel intercedes, God finds a ransom, and the result is padah — the soul is redeemed from the pit. The combination of kofer (ransom, v. 24) and padah (redemption, v. 28) creates a complete theology of ransomed deliverance.

verses.text_hebrew: הֶן־כׇּל־אֵ֭לֶּה יִפְעַל־אֵ֑ל פַּעֲמַ֖יִם שָׁל֣וֹשׁ עִם־גָּֽבֶר׃
verses.text_kjv: Lo, all these things worketh God oftentimes with man,
verses.rendering:
God does all these things
two or three times with a person —
translator_notes: Elihu now generalizes: hen kol elleh yif'al El ('indeed, all these things God does'). The pa'amayim shalosh ('two or three times') im gaver ('with a man') is another numerical ladder: God repeats this cycle of warning, suffering, intercession, and restoration multiple times. God does not give up after one attempt. Divine patience extends to repeated interventions. The word gever ('man, strong person') echoes 32:21 (gaver in the night of conception) — the same human being who was conceived in darkness can be rescued from the pit repeatedly.

verses.text_hebrew: לְהָשִׁ֣יב נַ֭פְשׁוֹ מִנִּי־שָׁ֑חַת לֵ֝א֗וֹר בְּא֣וֹר הַחַיִּֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: To bring back his soul from the pit, to be enlightened with the light of the living.
verses.rendering:
to bring his soul back from the pit,
to be illuminated by the light of the living.
verses.expanded_rendering: The purpose of God's repeated intervention: lehashiv nafsho minni shachat ('to bring back his soul from the pit'). The verb hashiv ('to bring back, to restore, to cause to return') from shuv is the great repentance/restoration verb. The destination of this return is magnificent: le'or be-or hachayyim ('to shine in the light of the living'). The or hachayyim ('the light of the living') is the world of the alive — the light that the living see, the light that the dead in Sheol do not see (Psalm 56:14). God's purpose in suffering is not destruction but rescue: to pull the soul back from death's edge into the radiant world of life. The shachat ('pit') and the or ('light') stand as the two poles of human existence, and God works relentlessly to move people from the first toward the second.
translator_notes: The phrase or hachayyim ('light of the living') appears also in Psalm 56:14 ('that I may walk before God in the light of the living'). It is the opposite of the tsalmaveth ('shadow of death') that Job invoked in 3:5 and 10:21-22. Elihu's vision of divine purpose ends in light, not darkness — the reversal of Job's opening curse.

verses.text_hebrew: הַקְשֵׁ֣ב אִ֭יּוֹב שְׁמַ֥ע לִ֑י הַ֝חֲרֵ֗שׁ וְאָנֹכִ֥י אֲדַבֵּֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: Mark well, O Job, hearken unto me: hold thy peace, and I will speak.
verses.rendering:
Pay attention, Job — listen to me.
Be silent, and let me speak.
translator_notes: Elihu returns to direct address: haqshev Iyov shema li ('pay attention, Job, hear me'). The imperative hachresh ('be silent, be still') from charash is a demand for Job's silence — Elihu has more to say. The ve-anokhi adabber ('and I myself will speak') uses the emphatic first-person pronoun anokhi rather than the common ani, adding weight: it is I who will speak.

verses.text_hebrew: אִם־יֵשׁ־מִלִּ֥ין הֲשִׁיבֵ֑נִי דַּ֝בֵּ֗ר כִּי־חָפַ֥צְתִּי צַדְּקֶֽךָּ׃
verses.text_kjv: If thou hast any thing to say, answer me: speak, for I desire to justify thee.
verses.rendering:
If you have words, answer me.
Speak — for I want to see you vindicated.
verses.expanded_rendering: Elihu offers Job the floor: im yesh millin hashiveni ('if you have words, answer me'). This is genuine invitation, not rhetorical trap — Elihu has pledged a fair hearing (33:5-7). But the reason for this offer is remarkable: ki chafatsti tsaddeqekha ('for I desire your vindication'). The verb chafats ('to desire, to delight in') combined with tsaddeqekha ('your justification, your vindication') means Elihu wants Job to be declared righteous. Unlike the three friends, who argued for Job's guilt, Elihu argues for a framework in which Job can be both innocent and suffering — a framework in which vindication is possible without requiring God to be unjust.
translator_notes: The phrase chafatsti tsaddeqekha ('I desire your vindication') sets Elihu apart from the friends. Eliphaz, Bildad, and Zophar all worked toward Job's condemnation. Elihu works toward Job's vindication — he just insists it must come through acknowledging God's sovereignty, not through accusing God of injustice.

verses.text_hebrew: אִם־אַ֭יִן אַתָּ֥ה שְׁמַֽע־לִ֑י הַ֝חֲרֵ֗שׁ וַאֲאַלֶּפְךָ֥ חׇכְמָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: If not, hearken unto me: hold thy peace, and I shall teach thee wisdom.
verses.rendering:
If not, listen to me.
Be silent, and I will teach you wisdom.
translator_notes: The closing verse: im ayin ('if not, if you have nothing to say') attah shema li ('you listen to me'). The final promise: va-aalefkha chokhmah ('and I will teach you wisdom'). The verb alaf ('to teach, to learn') is rare, adding formal weight. Elihu promises chokhmah ('wisdom') — the same wisdom he argued in 32:9 does not come automatically with age. He has positioned himself as wisdom's carrier, not by virtue of years but by virtue of the spirit (32:8). Whether he delivers on this promise is the question that chapters 34-37 will answer.
